did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning framework combining YOLOv8 for real-time skin lesion localization and a Swin Transformer for high-accuracy lesion classification on the HAM10000 dermatology dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel hybrid two-stage framework integrating YOLOv8 and Swin Transformer for skin cancer detection and classification. It details original experimental methodology, including dataset usage (HAM10000), model architecture, training procedures, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., mAP@0.5 of 92.2%, classification accuracy of 93.4%). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE Big Data), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection and Classification using Swin Transformer and YOLOv8
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Big Data
brief_summary: This study presents a hybrid two-stage deep learning framework for skin cancer detection and classification, integrating YOLOv8 for real-time lesion localization and a Swin Transformer for high-accuracy classification. The system was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven lesion categories. The YOLOv8 module achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP@0.5) of 92.2% for detection, while the Swin Transformer classifier attained a classification accuracy of 93.4%. The combined end-to-end pipeline demonstrated an overall accuracy of 91.1% with an inference speed of 28 FPS. These results indicate that the proposed architecture effectively balances diagnostic precision with real-time inference speed, making it suitable for clinical screening applications.

all_task_types.details: YOLOv8 module performs real-time lesion localization by predicting bounding boxes, objectness scores, and class probabilities for lesion candidates. Evaluated using mAP@0.5 (92.2%) and mAP@[0.5:0.95] (57.2%).

all_task_types.target: Lesion type categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign nevus)
all_task_types.details: Swin Transformer classifier predicts discrete diagnostic categories for localized regions of interest (ROIs). Evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset with seven lesion classes, achieving 93.4% classification accuracy and 92.8% macro F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary dataset for training and evaluating the proposed YOLOv8 and Swin Transformer framework.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the YOLOv8 detection module and the Swin Transformer classification module. The dataset was split into 70% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training. The dataset was split into 15% for validation.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of detection (mAP) and classification (accuracy, F1-score) performance. The dataset was split into 15% for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (e.g., accuracy, mAP, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.Notes: The YOLOv8-S model achieved stable convergence after approximately 70 epochs. The Swin Transformer classifier exhibited consistent improvement across epochs with smooth convergence of training and validation losses. The combined pipeline represents a >10% absolute improvement in diagnostic accuracy compared with an earlier CNN + YOLOv3 framework (which achieved 80% accuracy) while maintaining real-time operation. Minor confusion was observed between morphologically similar categories AKIEC and BCC.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid two-stage framework: YOLOv8 (for detection) and Swin Transformer (for classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: YOLOv8 initialized with COCO pre-trained weights; Swin Transformer initialized with ImageNet-1k pre-trained weights
architecture_used.input_size: 640x640 pixels for YOLOv8; 224x224 pixels for Swin Transformer

YOLOv8.loss_function: LCIoU (bounding box), Lobj_cls BCE (objectness), LBCE (class probability)

Swin Transformer.batch_size: Not explicitly reported (context mentions batch normalization)

classification_or_output_head.YOLOv8: Bounding-box coordinates, objectness scores, and class probabilities
classification_or_output_head.Swin Transformer: Lesion type classification (7 classes: NV, MEL, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC)

Data augmentation including random rotation, horizontal flipping, brightness jittering, and Gaussian noise
Normalization of lesion regions using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the training set

conclusions: The proposed hybrid two-stage framework integrating YOLOv8 for real-time lesion localization and a Swin Transformer for high-accuracy classification effectively balances diagnostic precision with inference speed.
The YOLOv8 detection module achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP@0.5) of 92.2% and maintained real-time inference speed of approximately 43 FPS on 640x640 input images.
The Swin Transformer classifier achieved a validation accuracy of 93.4% and a macro F1-score of 92.8% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The combined end-to-end pipeline demonstrated an overall accuracy of 91.1% and a macro F1-score of 90.2% while maintaining an inference speed of approximately 28 FPS.
The hybrid system represents a >10% absolute improvement in diagnostic accuracy compared to a previous CNN + YOLOv3 framework (which achieved 80% accuracy) while enabling true real-time operation.

The framework outperforms prior CNN-based systems both quantitatively and qualitatively, offering a favorable trade-off between speed and diagnostic precision suitable for clinical screening or handheld endoscopic systems.

Minor confusion was observed between morphologically similar categories, specifically Actinic Keratosis and Intraepithelial Carcinoma (AKIEC) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), suggesting that incorporating texture-level features or multimodal metadata could further enhance precision.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases (e.g., overrepresentation of fair-skinned individuals), limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse skin tones and global populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 test split, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific dataset's distribution.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the reported performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the improvements over baseline methods.

There is a notable discrepancy in the reported overall accuracy: the abstract and conclusion state 91.1%, while the results section text states '≈91%'; this lack of precision in reporting should be noted.

The comparison with prior work (CNN + YOLOv3 achieving 80% accuracy) is not a rigorous ablation within the same experimental setup but rather a citation of previous literature, which may not be directly comparable due to differences in preprocessing, data splits, or evaluation protocols.

The paper acknowledges minor confusion between Actinic Keratosis (AKIEC) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), indicating a limitation in distinguishing morphologically similar malignant lesions, which has critical clinical implications.
The hardware used for evaluation (Intel Xeon w3-2435 CPU and NVIDIA RTX A2000 GPU) is specific and may not reflect performance on other common clinical deployment hardware configurations.

The dataset split (70% train, 15% validation, 15% test) is described as stratified, but the exact number of samples per class in each split is not provided, preventing assessment of potential class imbalance in the test set.
